Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I felt like I had to do something, then cram in a few hours here and there. With six online courses, I tried to dedicate about three hours a day spread across the week, but it rarely worked out that neatly. I'm 27, so I've got other stuff going on in life that pulls my attention away from studying. Most of my actual learning happened late at night when things quieted down, listening to lectures or podcasts while doing other things.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due soon. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be putting in long hours because deadlines were approaching. Regular scheduling just doesn't work well for me with everything else I'm juggling. I tried to set a routine a few times, but it always fell apart after a week or two.
Q3: Usually it was a deadline notification or remembering that I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes a classmate would message me about an assignment and I'd realize I needed to log in and see what was happening. I wasn't one of those students who logged in daily just to browse around. It was more reactive than proactive, if I'm being honest.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became something I naturally incorporated into my day. There were brief stretches where I'd check it more regularly, usually when assignments were stacking up, but it never stuck as a habit. I think because I'm more of an auditory learner, I preferred downloading lectures and listening to them on my phone rather than sitting at the computer navigating the platform.
Q5: The recorded lectures were definitely the most useful for me. I could download them and listen while commuting or doing chores, which fit my auditory learning style perfectly. The peer support connections were also helpful when I actually used them—having other students explain things in their own words sometimes clicked better than the formal materials. I also appreciated when there were audio summaries of readings.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums entirely. You can see I posted zero times, and that's because typing out my thoughts in that format just doesn't work for how I learn. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings and optional videos. When you're low on resources and time, you have to prioritize what's going to directly help you pass, and those extras felt like a luxury I couldn't afford.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to an assignment or exam, it felt worth my time. Activities that seemed like busy work or weren't clearly connected to my grade just got pushed aside. I also valued things I could engage with passively, like listening while doing other tasks. At 27 with limited resources, I've learned to be pretty ruthless about where I put my energy.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like suggestions I could ignore, and I usually did. Required ones got my attention because they affected my grade, but even then I'd sometimes wait until the last possible moment. The optional stuff might have been valuable, but when you're already stretched thin and not super motivated, they're the first things to go. There's a mental calculation happening constantly about what's truly necessary.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture audio files and listen to them repeatedly—sometimes three or four times for difficult concepts. I also borrowed textbooks from the library since I couldn't afford to buy them all. A lot of my learning happened through conversations with peers outside the platform, just talking through concepts verbally since that's how information sticks for me.
Q10: There were many times I'd be listening to a downloaded lecture on a bus or while cooking, genuinely engaging with the material, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also meet up with a couple of classmates in person to talk through difficult topics, and those conversations were some of my most productive learning moments. The system only sees clicks and logins, not actual comprehension happening in the real world.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in once, download everything I needed for the week, then not touch the LMS again until the next batch of materials dropped. It was more efficient for me to have everything locally accessible. My internet isn't always reliable, and I couldn't afford to depend on being online to study. One download session could fuel a week of actual learning.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My stress levels were pretty high, and combined with low motivation, some weeks I just couldn't bring myself to care about the coursework. Life stuff outside of school—work pressures, financial stress—would sometimes make engaging with academic material feel almost impossible. Those were the weeks my attendance really suffered.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with financial stress, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to look at. Fatigue especially hit hard because I'd try to study at night and just couldn't focus on screen-based content. The competing responsibilities of being 27 and not having many resources meant that sometimes survival took priority over coursework. I'd use the LMS in quick bursts rather than sustained sessions.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd log in, scroll through some materials, maybe start a video, but my mind would be completely elsewhere. Going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd realize I'd been staring at the same page for ten minutes thinking about bills or other problems. The activity would register, but the learning definitely wasn't happening.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts indefinitely—obviously, since I never did any. I knew participating might help, but the format just felt wrong for how I learn and communicate. I also avoided some of the heavier reading assignments until absolutely necessary. Part of it was procrastination, part of it was knowing I could probably pass without doing everything perfectly. When motivation is low, you make strategic cuts.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage that I couldn't find otherwise. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the 48 hours before any major due date. Without deadlines, I honestly might not have logged in at all some weeks. They were the external pressure I needed since internal motivation wasn't cutting it.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect. Good feedback on assignments made me feel like maybe I was doing okay despite not being fully engaged, which honestly might have made me engage less. Critical feedback sometimes motivated me to try harder, but other times it just added to my stress and made me want to avoid the course. The peer support intervention helped a bit—having someone check in made me feel more accountable.
Q18: The structure gave me a framework to work within, but it didn't really pull me in. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me know what to prioritize, but the design assumed a level of consistent engagement I just couldn't maintain. Six courses simultaneously meant I was always triaging, deciding which course got attention that week. The structure helped me manage, but it didn't make me want to engage more.
Q19: The availability of downloadable audio content made engagement easier for my learning style. But the heavy emphasis on discussion forums and written participation made it harder—that's just not how I process or share information effectively. Also, the sheer volume of content across six courses was overwhelming. Simpler navigation and clearer priorities would have helped me focus my limited energy better.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something—maybe they're confused about what they need to do, or they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. Could also be that they're engaging with content but struggling to produce the output. Or maybe life keeps interrupting between accessing materials and finishing assignments. Activity doesn't equal productivity or understanding.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My attendance was under 50% and I barely touched discussions, but I still got a Distinction. I think it shows that some people learn effectively outside the system. I downloaded materials, studied offline, talked with peers in person, and showed up prepared for exams. The LMS activity doesn't capture that I was actually learning, just not in ways the platform could track.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, whether you meet deadlines. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're deeply focused or mindlessly scrolling. They definitely miss offline learning, peer conversations, and all the thinking that happens away from the screen. For auditory learners like me, a lot of real learning is invisible to the system.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's when you find yourself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when you're genuinely curious rather than just compliant. I'll be honest—I wasn't always engaged by that definition this semester. I did what I needed to do, but the deeper investment wasn't always there.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures while doing other things, or the conversations with classmates that actually helped concepts click. It wouldn't show my stress levels or how financial pressures affected my ability to focus. It also wouldn't capture that despite low motivation and moderate engagement, I actually learned the material well enough to score high on exams. The numbers tell a partial story at best.
